question: What is the definition of an automated message system under the Electronic Transactions Act?

request.question: What is the definition of an automated message system under the Electronic Transactions Act?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 26 of the Electronic Transactions Act, No. 19 of 2006, an 'automated message system' is defined as a computer program or an electronic or other automated means used to initiate an action or respond to data messages or performances in whole or in part, without review or intervention by a natural person each time an action is initiated or a response is generated by the system. [cite: 277]
notes: Ground truth verified against Section 26 interpretation clauses.

question: How is 'land' defined under the Registration of Documents Ordinance?

request.question: How is 'land' defined under the Registration of Documents Ordinance?

ground_truth_answer: According to Section 3 of the Registration of Documents Ordinance, 'land' includes things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth and any estate or interest in land, and a mortgage of or charge on land. [cite: 367]
notes: Ground truth must be checked against the preliminary chapter definitions.

question: What is the national minimum monthly wage for workers for the period commencing on April 1, 2025, and ending on December 31, 2025?

request.question: What is the national minimum monthly wage for workers for the period commencing on April 1, 2025, and ending on December 31, 2025?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 3(1)(a) of the National Minimum Wage of Workers Act, No. 3 of 2016 (as amended by Act No. 11 of 2025), the national minimum monthly wage for all workers in any industry or service is twenty-seven thousand rupees. [cite: 663]

expected_sources.title: National Minimum Wage of Workers (Amendment) Act, No. 11 of 2025

notes: Ensures the system captures the amended temporal clause correctly.

question: What is the presumed term of a verbal contract for the hire of a servant under the Service Contracts Ordinance?

request.question: What is the presumed term of a verbal contract for the hire of a servant under the Service Contracts Ordinance?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 3 of the Service Contracts Ordinance, a verbal contract for the hire of any servant (except for work usually performed by the day, by the job, or by the journey) is deemed in law to be a contract for hire and service for the period of one month, and is renewable from month to month. [cite: 691, 692]
notes: Evaluates simple statutory lookup for contract durations.

question: At what fixed monthly salary threshold does the protection against legal proceedings under the Public Servants (Liabilities) Ordinance cease to apply?

request.question: At what fixed monthly salary threshold does the protection against legal proceedings under the Public Servants (Liabilities) Ordinance cease to apply?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 2(2) of the Public Servants (Liabilities) Ordinance, the protection against legal actions does not apply to a public servant who, at the date the liability is contracted, is in receipt of a salary in regard to his fixed appointment of more than five hundred and twenty rupees a month. [cite: 773]
notes: Verifies exemption limits in public servant debt protection.

question: Does a violation of rights arising purely out of a contract constitute an infringement of fundamental rights under Article 12 of the Constitution?

request.question: Does a violation of rights arising purely out of a contract constitute an infringement of fundamental rights under Article 12 of the Constitution?

ground_truth_answer: No. According to Roberts v. Ratnayake [1986] 2 Sri L.R. 36, a violation of rights arising out of a contract does not constitute an infringement of the fundamental right to equality guaranteed under Article 12 of the Constitution.

expected_sources.title: Roberts and Another v. Ratnayake and Others

question: Can a third party sue partners to recover money for goods supplied without tendering a written Partnership Agreement into evidence?

request.question: Can a third party sue partners to recover money for goods supplied without tendering a written Partnership Agreement into evidence?

ground_truth_answer: Yes. In Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe [2008] 2 Sri L.R. 61, the Supreme Court held that under Section 18 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, third parties are not prevented from suing partners or offering evidence to prove a partnership exists, even in the absence of a written partnership agreement.

expected_sources.title: Samuel Gnanam and Others v. Ismail Lebbe

question: Does a Certificate of Sale issued by the National Savings Bank legally pass title to immovable property without a notarial conveyance?

request.question: Does a Certificate of Sale issued by the National Savings Bank legally pass title to immovable property without a notarial conveyance?

ground_truth_answer: Yes. According to Abeykoon v. National Savings Bank [1999] 3 Sri L.R. 144, Section 55 of the National Savings Bank Act contains express provisions for passing title via a Certificate of Sale. Applying the principle 'generalia specialibus non derogant', this specific statute takes precedence over the general requirement of notarial execution in Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance.

expected_sources.title: Abeykoon and Another v. National Savings Bank

question: When does the cause of action arise against a surety under a guarantee agreement for the purpose of computing prescription?

request.question: When does the cause of action arise against a surety under a guarantee agreement for the purpose of computing prescription?

ground_truth_answer: According to Hatton National Bank Limited v. Sellers Sports (Pvt) Ltd [2000] 3 Sri L.R., the obligation of sureties to make payment on a guarantee becomes effective only when a demand is made. Therefore, the cause of action arises, and the period of prescription under Section 6 of the Prescription Ordinance begins to run, at the time the demand is made and payment is refused.

expected_sources.title: Hatton National Bank Limited v. Sellers Sports (Pvt) Ltd and Others

question: Does the death of a mortgagee suspend the running of prescription on a mortgage bond if the rights devolve to minor children?

request.question: Does the death of a mortgagee suspend the running of prescription on a mortgage bond if the rights devolve to minor children?

ground_truth_answer: Yes. In Jayasena v. Dayasena [2002] 3 Sri L.R. 261, the court established that when a mortgagee's rights devolve onto minor children upon death, Sections 13 and 14 of the Prescription Ordinance come into operation. This statutory protection suspends the running of prescription until the disability of the children is removed, either by attaining majority or by death.

question: How does the Unfair Contract Terms Act differentiate between contract terms that exclude liability for negligence resulting in death or personal injury, versus those excluding liability for other types of loss or damage, and what legal standard must the latter satisfy?

request.question: How does the Unfair Contract Terms Act differentiate between contract terms that exclude liability for negligence resulting in death or personal injury, versus those excluding liability for other types of loss or damage, and what legal standard must the latter satisfy?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act No. 26 of 1997, any contract term or notice that purports to exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence is absolutely void and of no effect. However, under Section 3(2), a contract term or notice purporting to exclude or restrict liability for any other loss or damage resulting from negligence is not automatically void. Instead, it will only have legal effect in so far as such term or notice satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness'.

expected_sources.title: Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997

question: Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), what are the formal execution requirements for a transfer deed of immovable property, and how does the law accommodate a transferee who is a corporate body unable to be present?

request.question: Under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), what are the formal execution requirements for a transfer deed of immovable property, and how does the law accommodate a transferee who is a corporate body unable to be present?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 2(1) and 2(2) of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (as amended by Act No. 4 of 2024), a transfer deed for immovable property must be in writing, signed by every executant and the witnesses in the presence of a licensed notary public, all present at the same time and in the presence of one another. Crucially, both the transferor and transferee must affix their signatures and thumb impressions. If the transferee is a corporate body and its Board of Directors is unable to be present to execute the deed, the Board may authorize in writing any other person to sign and affix their thumb impression on the Board's behalf, provided that person complies with the simultaneous presence and attestation requirements of subsection (1).

expected_sources.title: Prevention of Frauds (Amendment) Act, No. 4 of 2024

question: Under the Sale of Goods Ordinance, how do the implied conditions for the quality or fitness of goods differ when a buyer relies on the seller's judgment for a specific purpose versus when the buyer simply purchases goods by description, and what exception applies if the buyer examines the goods?

request.question: Under the Sale of Goods Ordinance, how do the implied conditions for the quality or fitness of goods differ when a buyer relies on the seller's judgment for a specific purpose versus when the buyer simply purchases goods by description, and what exception applies if the buyer examines the goods?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 15 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, the general rule is 'caveat emptor', but two main exceptions apply. Under Section 15(1), if the buyer expressly or impliedly makes known the particular purpose for the goods—showing reliance on the seller's skill and judgment—and the seller deals in such goods, there is an implied condition that the goods are reasonably fit for that purpose. Conversely, under Section 15(2), if goods are bought by description from a dealer, there is an implied condition that they must be of 'merchantable quality'. However, a key exception applies to Section 15(2): if the buyer has actually examined the goods, the implied condition of merchantable quality does not apply to defects that such an examination ought to have revealed.

question: How does the Companies (Amendment) Act No. 12 of 2025 alter the legal status of existing bearer shares, and what are the specific obligations and penalties imposed on companies regarding the new 'beneficial ownership' disclosure?

request.question: How does the Companies (Amendment) Act No. 12 of 2025 alter the legal status of existing bearer shares, and what are the specific obligations and penalties imposed on companies regarding the new 'beneficial ownership' disclosure?

ground_truth_answer: The Companies (Amendment) Act No. 12 of 2025 strictly prohibits the issuance of bearer shares. For existing bearer shares, Section 51A mandates that holders must inform the company within 60 days of the Act's operation, provide their details, and convert them into registered shares; failure to comply results in the automatic nullification of the rights attached to those shares. Furthermore, new Sections 130A–130J introduce mandatory 'beneficial ownership' disclosures. Companies must identify natural persons holding 10% or more ownership or exercising effective control, maintain a register of these beneficial owners at their registered office, and submit the details to the Registrar. Non-compliance with these reporting and maintenance requirements constitutes an offence, rendering the company and responsible officers liable to a fine of up to Rs. 1 million, imprisonment for up to 10 years, or both.

question: How does the court synthesize the application of a condition subsequent in a deed of transfer with the formal requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance when the vendee has not signed the deed?

request.question: How does the court synthesize the application of a condition subsequent in a deed of transfer with the formal requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance when the vendee has not signed the deed?

ground_truth_answer: The court held that when a property is transferred with a condition subsequent attached to it, the condition cannot be disengaged from the property[cite: 85]. Even if the vendee did not sign the deed, accepting the encumbered title and consideration implies an obligation to retransfer[cite: 99, 142]. The vendee cannot approbate and reprobate by using section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance to avoid the obligation, as the provisions of the Ordinance cannot be used to effectuate a fraud[cite: 148, 149].

expected_sources.title: Gnanasambandam v. Bin Adaham and Another

question: How do the computation rules under Section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance interact with the two-year limitation period for tort actions prescribed by Section 9 of the Prescription Ordinance when the last day of the period falls on a weekend?

request.question: How do the computation rules under Section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance interact with the two-year limitation period for tort actions prescribed by Section 9 of the Prescription Ordinance when the last day of the period falls on a weekend?

ground_truth_answer: Section 9 of the Prescription Ordinance requires an action to be filed within two years "from" the time the cause of action arose[cite: 231]. Applying interpretation rules, the word "from" means the computation of the two-year period commences after excluding the day on which the cause of action accrued[cite: 234]. Furthermore, under Section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance, if the last day of the prescriptive period is a public holiday and the Court office is closed, the action is validly instituted if filed on the next working day[cite: 249, 251].

question: How do the requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance intersect with the licensing requirements of the Notaries Ordinance to determine the validity of a deed of transfer, and what is the effect on its registration?

request.question: How do the requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance intersect with the licensing requirements of the Notaries Ordinance to determine the validity of a deed of transfer, and what is the effect on its registration?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, a transfer of immovable property must be signed in the presence of a licensed notary and two witnesses present at the same time[cite: 364, 368]. If the attesting notary has ceased to practice and failed to obtain an annual license to officiate under Section 27(2) of the Notaries Ordinance, the deed is attested in violation of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance[cite: 374, 424]. Consequently, such a deed is of no force or avail in law and is subject to an order cancelling its registration under Section 31 of the Registration of Documents Ordinance[cite: 303, 425].

question: Under Section 37 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, what dual evidentiary burden must a seller discharge to hold a buyer liable for damages due to non-acceptance of delivery, particularly when goods are not physically in the agreed location?

request.question: Under Section 37 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, what dual evidentiary burden must a seller discharge to hold a buyer liable for damages due to non-acceptance of delivery, particularly when goods are not physically in the agreed location?

ground_truth_answer: Under Section 37 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, the seller must establish two elements: that the seller was ready and willing to deliver the goods, and that the seller explicitly requested the buyer to take delivery[cite: 540]. If the goods are not actually available in the seller's store at the time of delivery, the seller cannot be legally deemed ready and willing to deliver[cite: 543]. Without proving both physical readiness to deliver and an active request made to the buyer, the seller's claim for damages for non-acceptance fails[cite: 542, 544].

question: When assessing a buyer's failure to pay for prior instalment deliveries, what factors determine whether a seller is legally justified in withholding subsequent deliveries?

request.question: When assessing a buyer's failure to pay for prior instalment deliveries, what factors determine whether a seller is legally justified in withholding subsequent deliveries?

ground_truth_answer: The critical factor is not merely a failure to pay, but whether the circumstances demonstrate an intention by the buyer to repudiate the contract[cite: 625]. If the buyer's refusal to pay is conditional upon the seller accepting extra-contractual terms, such as demanding a guarantee against future loss, this constitutes a definite refusal to be bound by the contract[cite: 630, 631]. Such a repudiatory breach justifies the seller's refusal to make further deliveries[cite: 631].

question: How is the definition of 'goods' under the Sale of Goods Ordinance 1896 interpreted in relation to Roman-Dutch law classifications, and what is the impact on unwritten contracts for the sale of incorporeal rights?

request.question: How is the definition of 'goods' under the Sale of Goods Ordinance 1896 interpreted in relation to Roman-Dutch law classifications, and what is the impact on unwritten contracts for the sale of incorporeal rights?

ground_truth_answer: While Roman-Dutch law deems certain incorporeal rights (like debts or actions) as movable property, the Sale of Goods Ordinance defines "goods" strictly as tangible or corporeal movables capable of delivery[cite: 684, 697, 711]. An incorporeal right, such as a government monopoly or "arrack rent," does not constitute "goods" under the Ordinance[cite: 703, 709, 714]. Therefore, a contract for the sale of such a right is not governed by Section 4 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance and is valid and enforceable even if not made in writing[cite: 643, 732].

question: Kamal verbally agrees to sell his commercial property to Nimal for Rs. 50 Million. Nimal pays a Rs. 5 Million advance, and they shake hands on the deal in front of several witnesses. A month later, Kamal changes his mind, refuses to transfer the property, and refuses to return the advance. Nimal wishes to sue Kamal to force the sale of the property. Based on the formal requirements of the law, what legal hurdles does Nimal face in enforcing this contract?

request.question: Kamal verbally agrees to sell his commercial property to Nimal for Rs. 50 Million. Nimal pays a Rs. 5 Million advance, and they shake hands on the deal in front of several witnesses. A month later, Kamal changes his mind, refuses to transfer the property, and refuses to return the advance. Nimal wishes to sue Kamal to force the sale of the property. Based on the formal requirements of the law, what legal hurdles does Nimal face in enforcing this contract?

ground_truth_answer:
Issue: Whether a verbal agreement for the sale of immovable property is legally enforceable for specific performance.

Rule: Under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, no promise, bargain, contract, or agreement for establishing any security, interest, or encumbrance affecting land or other immovable property shall be of force or avail in law unless it is in writing and signed by the party making it (or by a person lawfully authorized) in the presence of a licensed notary public and two or more credible witnesses, and duly attested.

Application: The agreement between Kamal and Nimal for the sale of the commercial property was entirely verbal. Despite the payment of an advance and the presence of witnesses, the strict formal requirement of a notarial execution was not met. Therefore, the contract is statutorily void and of no force or avail in law.

Conclusion: Nimal cannot legally enforce the sale of the property (specific performance) because the verbal contract violates the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. (Note: While Nimal may have a separate quasi-contractual claim in unjust enrichment to recover the advance, the contract of sale itself is unenforceable.)

question: A consumer hires a logistics company to transport valuable fragile goods. The standard form contract provided by the company contains a clause stating: 'The company accepts absolute zero liability for any loss or damage to goods in transit, even if caused by the direct negligence of our employees.' The goods are destroyed because the driver negligently crashed the truck while texting. The company points to the contract clause to deny the claim. How should a court evaluate the validity of this exclusion clause?

request.question: A consumer hires a logistics company to transport valuable fragile goods. The standard form contract provided by the company contains a clause stating: 'The company accepts absolute zero liability for any loss or damage to goods in transit, even if caused by the direct negligence of our employees.' The goods are destroyed because the driver negligently crashed the truck while texting. The company points to the contract clause to deny the claim. How should a court evaluate the validity of this exclusion clause?

ground_truth_answer:
Issue: Whether a standard contract term excluding liability for property damage caused by negligence is legally valid and enforceable against a consumer.

Rule: Under Section 3(2) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997, a person cannot by reference to any contract term or to a notice given to persons generally or to particular persons exclude or restrict his liability for negligence resulting in loss or damage (other than death or personal injury) except in so far as the term or notice satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness'. 

Application: The logistics company is attempting to use a blanket exclusion clause in a standard form contract to avoid liability for property damage caused directly by their employee's negligence. The court will not automatically enforce this clause. Instead, the court must subject the clause to the 'requirement of reasonableness'. Given that it is a sweeping exemption in a standard form contract offered to a consumer, it places an unfair burden on the consumer and is highly likely to be deemed unreasonable.

Conclusion: The exclusion clause is not automatically void, but it will only be effective if it passes the reasonableness test. Under these facts, a court is highly likely to strike down the clause as unreasonable, thereby holding the logistics company liable for the negligently destroyed goods.

expected_sources.title: Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997

question: A local farmer orders 100 meters of specialized irrigation hosing from an agricultural supply dealer, explicitly stating to the dealer that the hosing must be able to withstand high-pressure water pumps used in mountainous terrain. The dealer recommends and sells a specific brand. Upon installation, the hoses immediately burst under the high pressure. The dealer refuses a refund, claiming the goods were 'merchantable' as they work fine for standard low-pressure farming. What legal argument can the farmer make to return the goods and get a refund?

request.question: A local farmer orders 100 meters of specialized irrigation hosing from an agricultural supply dealer, explicitly stating to the dealer that the hosing must be able to withstand high-pressure water pumps used in mountainous terrain. The dealer recommends and sells a specific brand. Upon installation, the hoses immediately burst under the high pressure. The dealer refuses a refund, claiming the goods were 'merchantable' as they work fine for standard low-pressure farming. What legal argument can the farmer make to return the goods and get a refund?

ground_truth_answer:
Issue: Whether there is a breach of an implied condition of fitness for a particular purpose when goods are merchantable for general use but fail for the buyer's specifically communicated purpose.

Rule: Under Section 15(1) of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, where the buyer, expressly or by implication, makes known to the seller the particular purpose for which the goods are required, so as to show that the buyer relies on the seller's skill or judgment, and the goods are of a description which it is in the course of the seller's business to supply, there is an implied condition that the goods shall be reasonably fit for such purpose.

Application: The farmer expressly communicated the particular purpose (withstanding high-pressure water pumps in mountainous terrain) to the agricultural dealer. By asking for the dealer's recommendation, the farmer relied on the dealer's skill and judgment. The dealer supplied the hosing in the ordinary course of business. Although the hosing might be merchantable for standard farming (under Section 15(2)), it breached the specific implied condition of fitness for the communicated high-pressure purpose under Section 15(1).

Conclusion: The farmer can successfully argue that the dealer breached the implied condition of fitness for a particular purpose. Consequently, the farmer is legally entitled to reject the goods, treat the contract as repudiated, and claim a refund.

question: Company A places a bulk order for raw materials by submitting an electronic form on Supplier B's website. Supplier B's 'automated message system' instantly processes the form and sends an email confirming the order and binding the supplier to a delivery date. The next day, human managers at Supplier B realize the market price has spiked and want to cancel the order, arguing that the contract is invalid because no human at Supplier B ever reviewed or accepted the order manually. Is Supplier B's argument legally sound?

request.question: Company A places a bulk order for raw materials by submitting an electronic form on Supplier B's website. Supplier B's 'automated message system' instantly processes the form and sends an email confirming the order and binding the supplier to a delivery date. The next day, human managers at Supplier B realize the market price has spiked and want to cancel the order, arguing that the contract is invalid because no human at Supplier B ever reviewed or accepted the order manually. Is Supplier B's argument legally sound?

ground_truth_answer:
Issue: Whether a contract formed without human review by an automated message system is legally valid and binding.

Rule: Under Section 11 of the Electronic Transactions Act, No. 19 of 2006, a contract shall not be denied legal validity or enforceability on the sole ground that it is in electronic form. Furthermore, Section 17(d) explicitly states that a contract formed by the interaction of an automated message system and a natural person, or by the interaction of automated message systems, shall not be denied validity or enforceability solely on the ground that there was no review or intervention by a natural person of the final contract or of each of the actions carried out by the automated message system.

Application: Company A initiated the contract, and Supplier B's programmed automated system accepted it and issued confirmation. Supplier B is attempting to deny validity precisely because a human manager did not intervene or review the transaction. This argument is directly prohibited by Section 17(d).

Conclusion: Supplier B's argument is not legally sound. The contract was validly formed through the automated message system, and Supplier B is legally bound to fulfill the order despite the lack of human intervention at the moment of acceptance.

question: On March 1st, Sunil sells a piece of land to Malik for valuable consideration and executes a valid notarial deed. Malik places the deed in his safe but forgets to register it at the Land Registry. Seeing an opportunity, Sunil fraudulently sells the exact same land to Nuwan on April 1st. Nuwan, who has no knowledge of the prior sale to Malik, pays full value and immediately registers his deed on April 2nd. Malik discovers this in May and claims the land belongs to him because he bought it first. Who has the superior legal title to the land?

request.question: On March 1st, Sunil sells a piece of land to Malik for valuable consideration and executes a valid notarial deed. Malik places the deed in his safe but forgets to register it at the Land Registry. Seeing an opportunity, Sunil fraudulently sells the exact same land to Nuwan on April 1st. Nuwan, who has no knowledge of the prior sale to Malik, pays full value and immediately registers his deed on April 2nd. Malik discovers this in May and claims the land belongs to him because he bought it first. Who has the superior legal title to the land?

ground_truth_answer:
Issue: Whether a subsequent registered deed executed for valuable consideration defeats a prior unregistered deed concerning the same immovable property.

Rule: Under Section 7(1) of the Registration of Documents Ordinance, an instrument executed or made on or after January 1, 1864, shall, unless duly registered, be void as against all parties claiming an adverse interest thereto on valuable consideration by virtue of any subsequent instrument which is duly registered. Section 7(2) adds that fraud or collusion in obtaining such subsequent instrument or in securing the prior registration thereof shall defeat the priority of the person claiming thereunder.

Application: Malik holds the prior instrument (March 1st) but failed to register it. Nuwan holds a subsequent instrument (April 1st) for which he paid valuable consideration, and he duly registered it. Because Nuwan acted in good faith with no knowledge of the prior sale, there is no fraud or collusion on his part in obtaining his deed or securing its registration. Therefore, under Section 7(1), Malik's unregistered deed becomes void as against Nuwan's subsequently registered deed.

Conclusion: Nuwan holds the superior legal title. Malik's deed is void against Nuwan due to his failure to register it, leaving Malik to seek a personal remedy against Sunil for fraud.

question: A vendor agrees to sell a commercial property to a buyer but subsequently negotiates to sell it to a third party for a higher price. The original buyer seeks an interim injunction to prevent the sale to the third party pending a specific performance suit. Based on Roberts v. Ratnayake, what legal standards must the buyer satisfy to obtain this equitable relief?

request.question: A vendor agrees to sell a commercial property to a buyer but subsequently negotiates to sell it to a third party for a higher price. The original buyer seeks an interim injunction to prevent the sale to the third party pending a specific performance suit. Based on Roberts v. Ratnayake, what legal standards must the buyer satisfy to obtain this equitable relief?

ground_truth_answer: Applying Roberts v. Ratnayake, the buyer must establish a prima facie case that there is a valid and enforceable contract for sale. Furthermore, the court will apply equitable principles, requiring the buyer to prove that damages would not be an adequate remedy and that the balance of convenience favors granting the injunction to maintain the status quo until the final determination of the specific performance action.

expected_sources.title: Roberts and Another v. Ratnayake and Others

question: A seller of agricultural produce verbally demands that a buyer take delivery by Friday. However, on that Friday, the goods are securely locked in a warehouse in a different district, not at the agreed place of delivery. If the seller sues for damages for non-acceptance, how would the court evaluate the seller's claim under Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai?

request.question: A seller of agricultural produce verbally demands that a buyer take delivery by Friday. However, on that Friday, the goods are securely locked in a warehouse in a different district, not at the agreed place of delivery. If the seller sues for damages for non-acceptance, how would the court evaluate the seller's claim under Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai?

ground_truth_answer: The court would likely dismiss the seller's claim. According to Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai, to succeed in an action for non-acceptance under the Sale of Goods Ordinance, the seller must prove both that a request was made to the buyer to take delivery AND that the seller was physically 'ready and willing' to deliver. Since the goods were located in a different district and not at the agreed delivery location, the seller was not legally ready and willing to perform, rendering the claim invalid.
relevant_sections: Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai Judgment Principles

question: A client’s cause of action for a tortious claim arose on October 10, 2022. The prescriptive period is exactly two years. October 10, 2024, falls on a declared public holiday when the court registry is closed. The client files the plaint on October 11, 2024. Has the action prescribed, according to the principles in Raja Nanayakkara v. Mercy Jayasinghe?

request.question: A client’s cause of action for a tortious claim arose on October 10, 2022. The prescriptive period is exactly two years. October 10, 2024, falls on a declared public holiday when the court registry is closed. The client files the plaint on October 11, 2024. Has the action prescribed, according to the principles in Raja Nanayakkara v. Mercy Jayasinghe?

ground_truth_answer: No, the action has not prescribed. Applying Raja Nanayakkara v. Mercy Jayasinghe, the computation of the prescriptive period excludes the day the cause of action accrued. Furthermore, interpreting this alongside the Interpretation Ordinance, if the final day of the prescriptive period falls on a public holiday when the court is closed, the period is extended, and filing on the immediate next working day (October 11) is deemed valid and within time.
relevant_sections: Raja Nanayakkara v. Mercy Jayasinghe Judgment Principles

question: An importer discovers that the machinery shipped by a foreign supplier is entirely defective and breaches the underlying sales contract. The importer immediately instructs their issuing bank to dishonor the irrevocable Letter of Credit. Under Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports, is the bank legally justified in withholding payment to the seller?

request.question: An importer discovers that the machinery shipped by a foreign supplier is entirely defective and breaches the underlying sales contract. The importer immediately instructs their issuing bank to dishonor the irrevocable Letter of Credit. Under Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports, is the bank legally justified in withholding payment to the seller?

ground_truth_answer: Generally, no. According to Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports, a Letter of Credit operates on the 'autonomy principle,' meaning the credit contract is independent of the underlying sales contract. Banks deal in documents, not goods. Unless the importer can establish clear, proven fraud by the beneficiary (the fraud exception) regarding the documents themselves, the bank is obligated to honor the credit if the presented documents strictly comply with the LC terms, regardless of the defective machinery.

expected_sources.title: Hatton National Bank Limited v. Sellers Sports (Pvt) Ltd

relevant_sections: Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports Judgment Principles

question: A purchaser enters into an agreement to buy a parcel of land. The vendor wrongfully repudiates the agreement. Instead of suing immediately, the purchaser waits six years before initiating an action for specific performance, during which time the land's value triples. Based on Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe, how should the court rule on the purchaser's claim for specific performance?

request.question: A purchaser enters into an agreement to buy a parcel of land. The vendor wrongfully repudiates the agreement. Instead of suing immediately, the purchaser waits six years before initiating an action for specific performance, during which time the land's value triples. Based on Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe, how should the court rule on the purchaser's claim for specific performance?

ground_truth_answer: The court should deny the claim for specific performance. Under Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe, specific performance is a discretionary, equitable remedy. A plaintiff must demonstrate that they have been consistently ready, willing, eager, and prompt to perform their obligations. The purchaser's unexplained delay of six years (laches), coupled with a drastic alteration in circumstances such as the tripled property value, defeats the equitable claim, though the purchaser might still seek damages for breach of contract.

expected_sources.title: Samuel Gnanam and Others v. Ismail Lebbe

relevant_sections: Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe Judgment Principles

question: Draft a formal written complaint to the Consumer Affairs Authority on behalf of a consumer who purchased a defective refrigerator, citing the relevant provisions for the Authority to inquire into the matter.

request.question: Draft a formal written complaint to the Consumer Affairs Authority on behalf of a consumer who purchased a defective refrigerator, citing the relevant provisions for the Authority to inquire into the matter.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a structured formal complaint letter addressed to the Director-General of the Consumer Affairs Authority. The draft should include placeholders for consumer details, trader details, and purchase dates, and explicitly request an inquiry under Section 13 of the Consumer Affairs Authority Act No. 9 of 2003 regarding the sale of defective goods.

expected_sources.title: Consumer Affairs Authority Act, No. 9 of 2003

question: Draft a boilerplate clause for a commercial supply agreement that explicitly recognizes the legal validity of electronic signatures and contracts formed via automated message systems, in accordance with the Electronic Transactions Act.

request.question: Draft a boilerplate clause for a commercial supply agreement that explicitly recognizes the legal validity of electronic signatures and contracts formed via automated message systems, in accordance with the Electronic Transactions Act.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a legally sound boilerplate clause in markdown. The clause should explicitly state that the parties agree to be bound by electronic signatures (referencing Section 7) and automated message systems (referencing Section 17), and confirm that no part of the contract shall be denied validity solely because it is in electronic form (referencing Section 3 of the Electronic Transactions Act No. 19 of 2006).

question: Draft a formal notice of rejection to a seller, on behalf of a buyer, stating that the delivered goods do not correspond with the description and lack merchantable quality, citing the relevant sections of the Sale of Goods Ordinance.

request.question: Draft a formal notice of rejection to a seller, on behalf of a buyer, stating that the delivered goods do not correspond with the description and lack merchantable quality, citing the relevant sections of the Sale of Goods Ordinance.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a formal notice letter to the seller. It must include placeholders for the contract date, description of goods, and defect details. It should explicitly state the rejection of goods based on a breach of implied conditions relating to description and merchantable quality under Sections 14 and 15 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, demanding a refund or replacement.

question: Draft a contractual clause attempting to limit liability for property damage caused by negligence, along with a proviso acknowledging that its enforceability is subject to the 'requirement of reasonableness' under the Unfair Contract Terms Act.

request.question: Draft a contractual clause attempting to limit liability for property damage caused by negligence, along with a proviso acknowledging that its enforceability is subject to the 'requirement of reasonableness' under the Unfair Contract Terms Act.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a draft limitation of liability clause. Crucially, the draft must explicitly state that it does NOT exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence (to comply with Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act No. 26 of 1997). It should then draft the limitation for other loss/damage, including a qualifying statement that the clause applies only to the extent it satisfies the 'requirement of reasonableness' under Section 3(2) of the Act.

expected_sources.title: Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997

question: Draft the notarial attestation clause required for a deed transferring immovable property, ensuring it complies with the specific witness and signature requirements under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance.

request.question: Draft the notarial attestation clause required for a deed transferring immovable property, ensuring it complies with the specific witness and signature requirements under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a standard notarial attestation clause format in markdown. It must explicitly declare that the instrument was signed by the executant(s) and two or more credible witnesses, all being present at the same time and in the presence of one another and the licensed notary public, fulfilling the strict formal requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance.

question: Draft an agreement for the assignment of an exclusive 'arrack rent' monopoly, structuring it as the sale of an incorporeal right based on the principles outlined in Croos v. De Soysa.

request.question: Draft an agreement for the assignment of an exclusive 'arrack rent' monopoly, structuring it as the sale of an incorporeal right based on the principles outlined in Croos v. De Soysa.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a structured agreement in markdown, use a contract-style template, include placeholders for party/monopoly details, and accurately reflect that this is a sale of an incorporeal right rather than 'goods'.

question: Draft the jurisdiction and limitation averment paragraphs for a plaint in a tort action, explicitly pleading that the action is not prescribed because the final day of the two-year period fell on a public holiday, applying the computation rules from Raja Nanayakkara v. Mercy Jayasinghe.

request.question: Draft the jurisdiction and limitation averment paragraphs for a plaint in a tort action, explicitly pleading that the action is not prescribed because the final day of the two-year period fell on a public holiday, applying the computation rules from Raja Nanayakkara v. Mercy Jayasinghe.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce structured plaint averments in markdown, use a pleading-style template, include placeholders for dates/court details, and clearly articulate the computation of time under the Interpretation Ordinance and Prescription Ordinance.

question: Draft a letter of demand from a beneficiary (seller) to an issuing bank demanding immediate payment under an irrevocable Letter of Credit, asserting the autonomy principle from Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports after the bank wrongfully withheld payment due to the buyer's complaints about the shipped goods.

request.question: Draft a letter of demand from a beneficiary (seller) to an issuing bank demanding immediate payment under an irrevocable Letter of Credit, asserting the autonomy principle from Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports after the bank wrongfully withheld payment due to the buyer's complaints about the shipped goods.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a structured letter of demand in markdown, use a notice-style template, include placeholders for bank/LC details, and firmly assert the legal position that banks deal in documents, not goods.

question: Draft an affidavit supporting an application to cancel the registration of a deed of transfer, averring that the attesting notary did not possess a valid annual license at the time of execution, rendering the deed void under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance as held in Rohan de Soyza v. Jinendradasa.

request.question: Draft an affidavit supporting an application to cancel the registration of a deed of transfer, averring that the attesting notary did not possess a valid annual license at the time of execution, rendering the deed void under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance as held in Rohan de Soyza v. Jinendradasa.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a structured affidavit in markdown, use an affidavit-style template, include placeholders for the deponent/deed/notary details, and clearly state the legal grounds for invalidity and cancellation of registration.

question: Draft an Answer (pleading) for a defendant vendor resisting a suit for specific performance of a land sale agreement, specifically pleading the defenses of laches and the plaintiff's failure to maintain continuous readiness and willingness, relying on the principles in Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe.

request.question: Draft an Answer (pleading) for a defendant vendor resisting a suit for specific performance of a land sale agreement, specifically pleading the defenses of laches and the plaintiff's failure to maintain continuous readiness and willingness, relying on the principles in Samuel Gnanam v. Ismail Lebbe.

ground_truth_answer: [DRAFT_TEST] The system should produce a structured answer in markdown, use a pleading-style template, include placeholders for court/party details, and articulate equitable defenses of delay and lack of promptness.

question: Review this draft agreement for the sale of a commercial property and identify any critical execution or formal validity risks under Sri Lankan law.

request.question: Review this draft agreement for the sale of a commercial property and identify any critical execution or formal validity risks under Sri Lankan law.

ground_truth_answer: [REVIEW_TEST] The system should analyze the uploaded document and identify that the execution block only provides spaces for the buyer and seller signatures. It must flag this as a critical risk, explaining that under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, any agreement for the sale of immovable property is void and of no force in law unless signed in the presence of a licensed notary public and two or more credible witnesses, and duly attested.

question: Review this standard form logistics service contract and identify clauses that are legally void or highly vulnerable regarding liability exclusions.

request.question: Review this standard form logistics service contract and identify clauses that are legally void or highly vulnerable regarding liability exclusions.

ground_truth_answer: [REVIEW_TEST] The system should review the uploaded logistics contract and identify the limitation of liability clauses. It must specify that any clause attempting to exclude liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence is absolutely void under Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act. Furthermore, it should note that any clause excluding liability for property damage caused by negligence is not automatically valid, but subject to the 'requirement of reasonableness' under Section 3(2) of the Act.

expected_sources.title: Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997

question: Review this draft B2B supply agreement for industrial machinery and evaluate the risks for the buyer associated with the 'As-Is' and 'Waiver of Warranties' clauses.

request.question: Review this draft B2B supply agreement for industrial machinery and evaluate the risks for the buyer associated with the 'As-Is' and 'Waiver of Warranties' clauses.

ground_truth_answer: [REVIEW_TEST] The system should locate the 'As-Is' and warranty waiver provisions in the document. It should advise the buyer that accepting these clauses severely limits their statutory protections under the Sale of Goods Ordinance. Specifically, it should warn that the buyer might lose the implied conditions that the goods correspond with their description (Section 14), are of merchantable quality (Section 15(2)), and are reasonably fit for the specific industrial purpose communicated to the seller (Section 15(1)).

question: Review this electronics store's standard Terms of Sale and Warranty document and identify any conditions that violate statutory consumer rights regarding defective goods.

request.question: Review this electronics store's standard Terms of Sale and Warranty document and identify any conditions that violate statutory consumer rights regarding defective goods.

ground_truth_answer: [REVIEW_TEST] The system should review the terms of sale and flag any 'strictly no returns or refunds' policies. It must explain that such absolute bans contravene consumer rights, noting that under Section 13 of the Consumer Affairs Authority Act, the Director-General has the power to inquire into consumer complaints regarding the sale of defective goods, and can order the trader to pay compensation or replace the goods, superseding restrictive store policies.

expected_sources.title: Consumer Affairs Authority Act, No. 9 of 2003

question: Verify whether Section 15 of the Consumer Affairs Authority Act No. 9 of 2003 allows a trader to arbitrarily refuse to sell goods they have in their possession for trade.

request.question: Verify whether Section 15 of the Consumer Affairs Authority Act No. 9 of 2003 allows a trader to arbitrarily refuse to sell goods they have in their possession for trade.

ground_truth_answer: [VERIFY_TEST] The system should produce a verification-style answer such as CONFIRMED, PARTIALLY CORRECT, or UNCONFIRMED, based only on retrieved source text. It should conclude UNCONFIRMED or INCORRECT, noting that Section 15(1) explicitly states no trader shall refuse to sell such goods, though Section 15(2) outlines specific defenses where refusal is permitted.

expected_sources.title: Consumer Affairs Authority Act, No. 9 of 2003

question: Verify the claim that under Section 14 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, if a sale is by both sample and description, it is legally sufficient if the bulk of the goods corresponds only with the sample.

request.question: Verify the claim that under Section 14 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance, if a sale is by both sample and description, it is legally sufficient if the bulk of the goods corresponds only with the sample.

ground_truth_answer: [VERIFY_TEST] The system should produce a verification-style answer such as CONFIRMED, PARTIALLY CORRECT, or UNCONFIRMED, based only on retrieved source text. It should conclude INCORRECT, as Section 14 explicitly states it is not sufficient that the bulk of the goods corresponds with the sample if the goods do not also correspond with the description.

question: Verify if Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997 permits a contract term excluding liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence, provided it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.

request.question: Verify if Section 3(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997 permits a contract term excluding liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence, provided it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.

ground_truth_answer: [VERIFY_TEST] The system should produce a verification-style answer such as CONFIRMED, PARTIALLY CORRECT, or UNCONFIRMED, based only on retrieved source text. It should conclude UNCONFIRMED or INCORRECT, explaining that under Section 3(1), such terms are of absolutely no effect. The 'requirement of reasonableness' applies only to other loss or damage under Section 3(2).

expected_sources.title: Unfair Contract Terms Act, No. 26 of 1997

question: Verify whether the judgment in Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai confirms that a seller is entitled to damages for non-acceptance under Section 37 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance merely by proving they were ready and willing to deliver, without needing to explicitly request the buyer to take delivery.

request.question: Verify whether the judgment in Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai confirms that a seller is entitled to damages for non-acceptance under Section 37 of the Sale of Goods Ordinance merely by proving they were ready and willing to deliver, without needing to explicitly request the buyer to take delivery.

ground_truth_answer: [VERIFY_TEST] REFUTED. The system should identify that the court in Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai held that under Section 37, the seller must prove both that they were ready and willing to deliver the goods AND that they explicitly requested the buyer to take delivery.
relevant_sections: Rawanna & Co. v. Arunachapillai Judgment Principles

question: Verify if Rohan de Soyza v. Jinendradasa held that a deed of transfer is void and of no force or avail in law under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance if it is attested by a Notary Public who did not hold a valid annual license at the time of execution.

request.question: Verify if Rohan de Soyza v. Jinendradasa held that a deed of transfer is void and of no force or avail in law under Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance if it is attested by a Notary Public who did not hold a valid annual license at the time of execution.

ground_truth_answer: [VERIFY_TEST] CONFIRMED. The system should verify that the court held the deed invalid because the attesting notary lacked a valid annual practicing license as required by the Notaries Ordinance, thereby failing the requirements of Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance.
relevant_sections: Rohan de Soyza v. Jinendradasa Judgment Principles

question: Verify whether the judgment in Jayasena v. Dayasena establishes that a plaintiff can successfully claim the equitable remedy of specific performance even if they completely failed to fulfill their own financial obligations within the time limits stipulated in the agreement.

request.question: Verify whether the judgment in Jayasena v. Dayasena establishes that a plaintiff can successfully claim the equitable remedy of specific performance even if they completely failed to fulfill their own financial obligations within the time limits stipulated in the agreement.

ground_truth_answer: [VERIFY_TEST] REFUTED. The system should review the case and state that a party in breach, or one who fails to perform their part of the agreement (such as paying the consideration) within the required timeframe, is generally not entitled to the equitable remedy of specific performance.

question: Can you check if the bank is justified in withholding payment under this Letter of Credit based on the Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports case?

request.question: Can you check if the bank is justified in withholding payment under this Letter of Credit based on the Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports case?

ground_truth_answer: [CLARIFICATION_TEST] The system should ask the user to upload or provide the Letter of Credit or related correspondence they want reviewed against the Hatton National Bank v. Sellers Sports precedent.

question: Does my company's standard sales contract violate the Unfair Contract Terms Act regarding negligence?

request.question: Does my company's standard sales contract violate the Unfair Contract Terms Act regarding negligence?

ground_truth_answer: [CLARIFICATION_TEST] The system should ask the user to upload or attach the standard sales contract so that the liability exclusion clauses can be evaluated against the Unfair Contract Terms Act.

question: I bought some industrial machinery from a seller and it broke down. Can I reject the goods and get my money back under the Sale of Goods Ordinance?

request.question: I bought some industrial machinery from a seller and it broke down. Can I reject the goods and get my money back under the Sale of Goods Ordinance?

ground_truth_answer: [CLARIFICATION_TEST] The system should ask the user for more specific facts about the transaction, such as whether the buyer communicated a particular purpose for the machinery to the seller (Section 15(1)), whether the machinery was bought by description (Section 15(2)), and whether the buyer had examined the goods before purchase.
